data.title: Schwab Trades Above Resistance: Next Stop Is Late '21 All-Time Highs
data.description:
Charles Schwab (NYSE:SCHW) has been bumping its head on the January ’23 high of $86 and change which this blog’s technician (Gary Morrow, @garysmorrow over on X), has been saying is (or was) formidable technical resistance.

The all-time-high on Schwab was $95.62 in the first week of January ’22. From there the stock sank to lows below $60 in mid-2022, and then ran up in late ’22 to that previously mentioned mid-$80s level in early ’23 before the market figured out there was not going to be any recession (and hence no fed funds rate reductions) and the stock traded all the way down to the mid-$40s in mid-2023.

Here’s a look at Schwab’s total return versus the S&P 500’s total return from January 1, ’22 to May 14, ’25:

Schwab has given up 558 bps a year to the S&P 500s annual return in the last 3 years, but that could be ready to change.

Fundamental analysis

What struck me when updating SCHW’s valuation spreadsheet after the mid-April ’25 earnings report is the expected EPS growth for the next 3 years that the analyst community has assigned Schwab:

  • ’25: 31% EPS growth and +15% revenue growth
  • ’26: 20% EPS growth and +10% revenue growth
  • ’27: 16% EPS growth and +8% revenue growth

Looking at the estimates as of today (sourced from LSEG), the revenue estimates have been revised higher since mid-April ’25 or post the Q1 ’25 earnings release, while the EPS estimates are the exact same.

The stock – at $88 per share – is up $14 since the Q1 ’25 earnings release, trading at 21x EPS for expected 31% EPS growth. (Had to look at that twice, but the numbers are good.)

Schwab assets under management totaled $9.9 trillion as of March 31, ’25, second only to BlackRock (BLK), I believe.

The one note from the earnings call that stuck was that Schwab management noted that legacy TDAM (TD Ameritrade) clients were returning to Schwab, showing Q1 ’25 organic growth of 2-3%. Schwab thinks there is a long runway to recover that business.

Not mentioned at all, or only by the Treasury Secretary, is easier capital rules that will eventually be passed by Congress for banks and brokers – Jamie Dimon has really been beating the drum on this since January ’25 – which might allow Schwab to repurchase some stock and increase the dividend, which has been at $0.25 for the last 10 quarters.

Clients were long the stock in the late 1990s, in the halcyon days of day trading and E-trade commercials, rode some of the stock through the decade from 2000 to 2009 (painfully), and then started accumulating Schwab again after the worst decade of stock returns since the 1930s.

If Schwab takes out the $96 high, it’s likely going much higher, but it will benefit from an easier Fed and a favorable stock market in ’25, if they materialize.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. None of this information may be updated and, if updated, may not be done in a timely fashion. The EPS and revenue estimates of Schwab are sourced from LSEG.com.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: There Is Currently No Good Argument In Favor Of MicroStrategy
data.description:
Introduction

MicroStrategy Incorporated (NASDAQ: MSTR) is likely one of the most controversial stocks out there. While some love it and praise Michael Saylor's strategy, others hate it. In this article, I will show why MSTR is currently a bad investment and even a potential short candidate.

Fundamentals

MSTR's profitability is overall mixed. While the company does have a strong gross profit margin of 70%, far higher than the industry average, it also has a negative ROE of -30.86% and negative ROA of -12.14%, in contrast to positive values for the sector median.

Furthermore, growth does look far worse. MSTR currently has negative revenue growth of -6.19% and negative forward revenue growth of -1.31%. Additionally, forward EBITDA growth is at -25.86%, which does imply no great future outlook for the company. This is especially a problem as the rest of the industry shows positive growth numbers.

Considering the rather bad profitability and growth measures, MSTR's valuation looks completely irrational. At a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 3562.79, the company is more than 240 times as expensive as the median. While the P/B ratio is only 3.44, it still seems elevated considering that other companies that do buy and hold, like Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B), are only at a P/B ratio of 1.66. The P/S ratio is again extremely high at 193.9, 64 times higher than the median value. Furthermore, MSTR does not even have positive earnings yet, meaning that there is no P/E ratio at the moment.

Peer Group Analysis

While there are not many companies that have the exact same business model that MSTR has, it does make sense to compare MSTR to companies that are also heavily reliant on the performance of cryptos.

When looking at profitability, MSTR has a solid gross profit margin, with only Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) and Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD) being strong at 85.25% and 90.98%, respectively. Nevertheless, MSTR has by far the worst ROE and ROA, with MARA Holdings, Inc. (MARA) being the only other company that shows negative (but much less severe) returns at -10.61% ROE and -5.11% ROA.

Considering growth, every company from the peer group, including all the above-mentioned and Block, Inc. (XYZ), has positive current and forward revenue growth as well as positive forward EBITDA growth.

The last point is valuation, displayed in Figure 1, and also includes Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) as it is firstly a big holder of Bitcoin and secondly is known for having a high valuation, making it a good comparison to MSTR that shares those attributes. As can be seen, MSTR is by far the most expensive company in terms of EV/EBITDA and P/S ratios. It is almost 50 times as expensive as TSLA, one of the most hyped companies out there. That MSTR has a comparably low P/B ratio is likely due to its position as an asset holder. However, the comparison to MARA, which can be considered an asset holder, instantly shows that MSTR is even more expensive in this ratio.

All of this raises the question of how MSTR's high valuation can be justified if the company has the worst fundamentals at the highest price. This problem becomes even more severe when comparing the company to the business models of its peers. As a buy-and-hold Bitcoin holder, MSTR can only make money when Bitcoin rises. In contrast, COIN and HOOD make money as long as trading volume is high. Furthermore, MARA mines Bitcoin and is therefore also not completely reliant on Bitcoin rallying.

Another factor that has to be considered is MSTR's strategy for buying Bitcoin. To do so, the company issues convertible debt. Because of this, debt holders usually use their option to convert debt to equity as long as MicroStrategy's stock price rises, which again is dependent on how Bitcoin performs. This has so far allowed the company to maintain a relatively low debt/equity ratio of around 24%. Nevertheless, should Bitcoin stop rising for a certain time, debt holders will not convert, burdening MSTR with interest payments that they somehow have to finance. Furthermore, as one of the biggest buyers of Bitcoin over the past years, the question remains of how much of the price increase in Bitcoin can be attributed to MSTR. Should MSTR stop buying because of potential interest payments, we could see much slower price appreciation in the Bitcoin market, again increasing the debt level of MSTR, which could lead to a self-reinforcing spiral where MSTR would have to sell some of its 568,840 Bitcoins to finance its debt. While the company currently pays between 0.625% and 2.25% interest on its convertible debt, rates could spike if the call aspect of the debt is considered as being less valuable. All of this adds additional tail risk and further increases the question of why investors should not simply buy Bitcoin directly instead of buying it through MSTR at a premium of roughly 94% to the NAV of its Bitcoins (dividing the market cap of 114B by the value of Bitcoins held, which is $102,600 times 568,840).

Trade Execution

Resulting from all of this is that investors should at best stay away from MSTR stock due to its high valuation. While I generally like shorting the stock, I would only do so on the way down, when it either falls below its 200-day simple moving average or turns to negative 6-month momentum. Currently, the company is far away from both of these suggestions. While it could also be interesting to short MSTR and go long on Bitcoin, the trade could show some losses as MSTR is generally even more volatile than Bitcoin. Nevertheless, it could be an attractive way to profit from a fall in P/B value despite rising crypto prices.

Conclusion

In total, MSTR seems like the fundamentally weakest stock in the whole crypto sector at an incredibly high valuation that simply suggests that the company is overhyped. Furthermore, MSTR is less adaptable than other companies from the crypto sector, while at the same time trading at a huge premium to the Bitcoin it holds, raising the question of why investors should not simply invest in Bitcoin directly. Because of this, the company gets a “strong sell” from me as I simply do not see any credible argument in favor of MSTR when comparing it to valid alternatives.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Applied Materials Earnings: This Stock Remains A Buy
data.description:
Applied Materials, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMAT) reported Q2 2025 earnings after the close Thursday in the midst of pervasive uncertainty regarding the semiconductor market worldwide and regarding how economic conditions will shake out going forward. Despite rebounding from the worst of the tariff lows, AMAT remains in a precarious position because of these uncertainties. That said, I think long-term investors should continue to tune out the noise and hold this stock for a long time.

It has been a wild couple months for AMAT and for the market as a whole, as the stock fell into the $120 range during the worst of the tariff war woes but has since seen a pronounced recovery, along with the rest of the market. Shares now trade around where they did following the release of the previous earnings report back in February.

The day after that Q1 report, I wrote an article looking at the results and concluded that the company's steady growth, stable margins, and technological leadership made it a solid stock to own for the long-term. That article can be read here. Comparing prices then and now, AMAT is mostly unchanged while the S&P 500 (SP500) is down around 3%.

Depending on how the stock opens tomorrow, these numbers might look a bit different, as AMAT is currently down around 5% as I write this. It's hard to pinpoint exactly why, considering revenue growth and earnings were higher than estimates and the Q3 guide looks solid, but I often find that looking for meaning in the price movements post earnings is a fool's errand. Let's focus on the results and go from there.

Here's a quick recap from the earnings presentation:

Really across the board, Q2 was a strong quarter and demonstrated the core strengths of the company: revenue grew steadily in the mid single-digits YoY (7%), margins improved incrementally (+170 bps to gross margin), and EPS jumped to a new record. Unlike the other semiconductor high flyers like Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO), Applied Materials has a much more gradual and steady progression that follows the long, drawn-out process of building fab capacity years in advance.

This lengthy production timeline is likely one of the reasons that CFO Brice Hill was able to provide the following update:

Despite the dynamic economic and trade environment, we have not seen significant changes to customer demand and are well-equipped to navigate evolving conditions with our robust global supply chain and diversified manufacturing footprint.

With uncertainty around every corner, especially for companies with global supply chains like Applied Materials, these statements are important. While economic uncertainty is something companies take into account, secular demand trends are typically strong enough and have enough staying power to outlast a trade spat. As such, companies like Applied Materials and the enterprises that purchase its equipment for their fab build-outs, usually go ahead with their timelines and tune out the politicians.

To back up this notion, the company issued Q3 2025 guidance of, at the midpoint, $7.2 billion revenue (+6.2% YoY), $2.35 non-GAAP EPS (+10.8% YoY), and gross margin of 48.3% (+90 bps YoY). Again, we can see the company excelling in incremental improvement across the board.

If I had to guess why the stock was selling off after hours, I'd wager the slight sequential decline in gross margin might have something to do with it, but, again, I put more stock in the numbers themselves rather than the market reaction.

With trade uncertainty as high as it is, these numbers are, in my opinion, fairly reassuring to investors. Much of Applied Materials' current revenue growth is driven by AI demand and the infrastructure build-outs for both Foundry-Logic as Nvidia, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and custom accelerator vendors design and ramp new AI chips at manufacturers like TSMC (TSM), and in DRAM as DDR5 and HBM continue to ramp, and as DDR6 nears its launch date in late 2025/early 2026. That the company continues to project strength in this area when there were growing concerns about AI expenditures at hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN), indicates the current uncertainty is not deterring end customers.

As for cash flow, Applied Materials continues to leverage its rising revenue and margin expansion to generate cash flow and give capital back to investors. The company paid out $325 million in dividends and repurchased nearly $1.7 billion in stock during the quarter, which is already shaping up to have been a savvy move. Free cash flow for the quarter was $1.06 billion, which is well above the figure for Q1 2025 but below the result for Q4 2024 as the metric continues to be lumpy due to one-time charges and fluctuations in capital expenditures. However, the company's balance sheet remains strong with $6.7 billion in cash & equivalents & short-term investments, $5.4 billion in long-term debt, and a payout ratio of just 30%, leaving plenty of room for continued increases.

I'd also like to again highlight the success of the company's Applied Global Services segment, which has grown to $1.56 billion in quarterly revenue and, at a 28.5% operating margin, earns more than enough on its own to cover the dividend. Applied Materials has a strong moat, is a cash generating machine, and continues to cut costs and find margin improvement while serving a growing and essential global market for cutting-edge chips. This quarter demonstrated that yet again.

As we have all seen in recent months, there are, of course, risks here. While the percentage of revenue derived from China has fallen from 43% in Q2 2024 to 25% in Q2 2025, this remains an area of exposure for the company. The current U.S.-China tariff detente still calls for 30% tariffs and this could pose a major risk of disrupting the company's supply chain and margins, not to mention the possibility that negotiations stall and trade conditions deteriorate.

However, I think investors should look past the near-term and take a long view of the company, the industry, and the global economy. Semiconductors are the lifeblood of the technology sector and, if AI has shown us anything, it's that processing power and cutting-edge datacenters will be vital to companies' and countries' economic futures. Applied Materials is perfectly positioned to continue to sell shovels in this gold rush, the next one, and the next five after that.

Investor Takeaway

Despite falling close to 5% after reporting Q2 2025 earnings results, I think AMAT remains a compelling long-term play for investors that target a strong moat and revenue derived from strong secular growth trends. The company's cutting-edge foundry, logic, and DRAM offerings continue to drive incremental revenue growth and margin improvement even in an uncertain economic environment.

If the stock opens down tomorrow, I think investors should consider adding to long-term positions. I expect there will be some volatility in the short-term as investors digest the earnings news and as the market perhaps cools off what has been an incredibly hot rally the last month. But for those looking a decade or two into the future, I continue to see AMAT as a Buy.

Thanks for reading and best of luck!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Great Time To Buy SPXL
data.description:
Introduction

A few months ago, I already published an article about the market-beating Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA: SPXL) strategy that I use. Since some requested an update on the strategy, I will talk about the strategy's performance since my last article. Also, I will show why now seems to be a very good time to buy SPXL and get that extra leverage on SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA: SPY).

Key ETF Data

SPXL is a leveraged ETF that aims to achieve 3 times the daily return of SPY. While it currently has an expense ratio of 0.87%, it also pays a dividend of 0.91%, canceling out the cost. While the expense ratio is high, the bid/ask spread is very low at 0.04%, making it a good candidate for a more active strategy. Generally speaking, the ETF is very liquid at a daily share volume of more than 6 million.

Performance Update

To remind you, the strategy I am referring to is to hold SPXL when SPY is above its 200-day simple moving average and sell the position when SPY falls below this threshold. Since my last post, the strategy has generally underperformed, which is normal as it is more volatile, even with the stop loss aspect. Nevertheless, the strategy did great in navigating tail risk. On March 26, it switched to being market neutral, leading to not being affected by the big sell-off on Trump's “liberation day”. However, since the market had already fallen a bit before that, the strategy was still down 17% until this point. Nevertheless, this continued to be its maximum drawdown, while holding SPXL without any strategy resulted in losses of up to 42.37%.

Right now, the strategy is slightly underperforming SPXL year to date due to the strong recovery that has happened over the past few weeks. The reason for this is simply that what we have just seen is likely one of the worst scenarios that can happen for the strategy. Figure 1 shows the daily performance chart of SPY as well as its simple moving average. As can be seen, the moving average did not move downward with the market, since the recovery was very quick. This, in combination with SPY breaking the 200-day moving average by rising 3% from close to opening, led to a higher price for re-entering the position than for selling it. While this is unlucky, the performance difference is only marginal at around 1-2%.

Current Signal And Outlook

Just on Monday, the signal has now finally changed back to buy for SPXL. From a historical perspective, this means lower volatility and higher return in SPY, as can be seen in Figure 2 and Figure 3, showcasing that the S&P 500 index has almost half the volatility above the 200-day moving average that it normally has below it. Furthermore, they also show the annualized return of more than 10%, while the ETF generally has a negative excess return below the barrier. The lower volatility is especially important due to volatility drag. When SPY has a down day of -20% followed by an up day of +25%, it is back at its base level. However, SPXL would fall by 60% to gain 75% on the next day. This would culminate in a loss of 30%, which can lead to losses along the way, depending on how high the volatility is.

This is also reflected in the current VIX level (Figure 4) of only 19.76, slightly below the important barrier of 20 that signals increased volatility. Furthermore, remains at a positive 1-year momentum, while only being 2% away from shifting towards an also positive 6-month momentum. All of this, as well as less trade tensions, should further allow the index to rise at lower volatility, making SPXL an even better option to bet on than SPY.

Risks

As always, Leveraged ETFs like SPXL carry much higher risks and are not suitable options for risk-averse investors. It is important for you to read the fund's prospectus and SEC's related article to gain a full understanding of these risks. Another current risk could be that SPY will move around the 200-day moving average, causing a lot of buy and sell signals and thereby transaction costs. While this is unlucky, it is the only big drawdown of the strategy that has achieved an average annualized return of over 20% over almost 100 years now. Also, waiting out and only entering the position later on could be a huge mistake, as the ETF can move up 9% on a single 3% plus day in SPY, which would cost a lot of performance.

Conclusion

While geopolitics remain volatile, SPXL still looks like a great buy for risk-friendly investors at the moment, as multiple indicators suggest lower volatility and higher returns in the stock market. Nevertheless, investors should expect some transaction costs along the way, should SPY continue to remain close to the 200-day simple moving average.

data.title: New Fortress Energy: Sell On Increased Likelihood Of Near-Term Debt Restructuring
data.description:
Note:

I have covered New Fortress Energy Inc. or "NFE" (NASDAQ:NFE) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Seven months ago, I upgraded my rating on New Fortress Energy from "Sell" to "Hold" after the company successfully addressed near-term debt and liquidity issues.

However, the subsequent relief rally in New Fortress Energy's common shares turned out to be short-lived as market participants remained wary of the company's poor financial condition:

Even the recent sale of NFE's Jamaican operations to Excelerate Energy (EE) for estimated net proceeds of $778 million has not been sufficient to address persistent debt issues.

As a result, the company has been required to seek near-term covenant relief from lenders in exchange for higher interest payments.

However, following NFE's dismal Q1/2025 earnings report, odds of the company violating debt covenants by the end of Q3 have increased.

Both revenues and profitability were a far cry from consensus expectations.

On the conference call, management attributed the shortfall to the absence of anticipated one-time benefits like a recently negotiated $110 million lump sum payment related to its gas supply contract with the Puerto Rico Electric Power Authority ("PREPA").

However, according to a Deutsche Bank analyst note, payment has been held back due to a proposed amendment which "includes certain guarantees in the case of insolvency," which isn't exactly reassuring:

In addition, the company expected to monetize at least one FSRU sub-charter contract during the quarter but based on management's statements on the conference call, decided to sweeten the deal with additional sub-charters:

(...) we think that combining this with the other charters that have been signed as well as ones in process makes this more attractive as a package of contracts than one-off.

In total, NFE expects to add approximately $200 million in liquidity from the monetization of up to four FSRU sub-charters.

Moreover, the company still expects a favorable near-term resolution of its $659 million request for equitable adjustment with the Federal Emergency Management Agency ("FEMA") following the surprise termination of a high-margin emergency power supply contract in Puerto Rico last year. However, the amount and timing of a potential settlement remain entirely unclear at this point.

In combination with other expected one-time gains, Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 is now expected in a range of $1.25 billion to $1.50 billion, up from management's most recent $1 billion guidance. However, these numbers are not comparable as the $1 billion guidance excluded the FEMA claim.

In fact, core Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be below $600 million this year, well below the $781 million recorded last year:

Please note that achieving this target will require substantial contributions from assets in Brazil that have yet to commence commercial operations.

On a more positive note, recent amendments to the company's credit facilities should boost near-term liquidity which, at least in theory, could be utilized to repurchase some of the company's near-term debt maturities at a substantial discount to face value:

Please note that NFE's longer-dated maturities are trading at even more distressed levels. For example, the company's recently issued 12% 2029 superpriority notes are now changing hands around 40% of par value:

Clearly, market participants are expecting the worst and quite frankly, I can't blame them as the company has overpromised and underdelivered basically ever since going public in early 2019, with profitability often missing management's ambitious projections by a wide margin.

In addition, the ill-advised adoption of an overly generous dividend policy and resulting payment of a $626 million special dividend in early 2023 has weakened the company's financial position substantially.

Moreover, poor execution has resulted in delays and cost overruns for key projects like NFE's new Fast LNG facility offshore Mexico.

Based on statements made by management during the questions-and-answers session of the conference call, the company appears to have paused construction of its second Fast LNG facility, likely due to its financial woes. NFE even decided to exclude this key asset from its earnings presentation. Kudos to Capital One analyst Wade Suki for asking the right questions on the call.

Pro forma for the net proceeds from the Jamaica sale, liquidity at quarter-end amounted to approximately $1.1 billion. However, commitments under the company's $900 million revolving credit facility will be reduced to $630 million by the end of the third quarter. Adjusted for this reduction, pro forma liquidity calculates to $841 million.

With recent debt restructuring efforts having resulted in substantially higher interest payments, cash flow is likely to remain negative in the current quarter.

While management continues to advertise potential asset level financings as a solution to NFE's financial woes, I don't expect potential counterparties to engage with a company seemingly teetering on the brink of bankruptcy.

Even after Thursday's 65%+ selloff in the company's shares, I wouldn't touch the common equity with a ten foot pole given the very real risk of a near-term debt restructuring. With even NFE's superpriority debtholders expecting a major haircut, equity holders would likely end up with nothing.

As a result, speculative investors should rather focus on the company's bonds which should see at least some recovery in bankruptcy.

Risks

As usual, the bear thesis is not without risks. While odds seem to be stacked firmly against the company, there are a still number of potential catalysts for the shares:

  1. Favorable resolution of the company's $659 million FEMA claim
  2. Successful monetization of FSRU sub-charters
  3. Receipt of the proposed PREPA settlement
  4. Surprise asset level financing.

However, I do not expect any of these potential catalysts to play out in the near-term. Please note also that 1-3 are already included in the company's guidance for 2025.

Bottom Line

New Fortress Energy reported abysmal quarterly results which triggered a massive selloff in the company's securities as market participants are seemingly preparing for the worst.

While the company has secured covenant relief for the current quarter, further credit facility amendments might be required to avoid violating debt covenants by the end of Q3.

For my part, I would not be surprised to see the company entering into forbearance agreements with lenders in the not-too-distant future in order to negotiate a comprehensive debt restructuring which would likely be implemented under chapter 11.

With billions of debt ranking well ahead of the common equity, shareholders are likely to end up with nothing in a potential bankruptcy.

Consequently, I am lowering my rating from "Hold" to "Sell."

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Abel Might Not Be Able To Ride The AI Wave
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B), the stock has gained 1.6% in price.

The AI-driven, innovation-centric economy will be testing the limits of Buffett and Berkshire's traditional value investing philosophy. There is immense uncertainty entering the markets right now, which only the most agile will likely be able to tolerate and outperform in. Buffett has always generally avoided cutting-edge tech sectors, admitting they lie outside his "circle of competence." His conservative stance in markets like insurance, banking, energy, and consumer goods where cash flows are tangible and easier to forecast protected him from many tech bubble bursts but also has meant missing some big opportunities—the largest of which will be right now, with AI. Buffett has to be admired. Indeed, it is not without reason that he is often called "the greatest of all time". However, Buffett's focus on present-day value leads him to heavily discount future growth potential. In the current exponential age of technology, that growth is the biggest source of outsized returns. Not many will be beating the major indexes without it, and certainly not the Nasdaq-100 (NDX) (QQQ). Buffett's framework excels at protecting capital and was, at one point, the best compounding machine the market knew for long-term durability. However, in this nascent age, it under-appreciates the option value of transformative innovation. Buffett's principles retain their wisdom, and anti-speculation remains prudent, but the locus of value creation is shifting. The onus is on the new CEO, Greg Abel, to enlarge Berkshire's circle of competence to include the digital economy or risk fading away into obscurity as a legacy alpha fund with low returns from here on out. The core risk for Berkshire is that there will be a widening conceptual gap between where value is created and what management are willing (or able) to invest in.

Financial Implications: Berkshire vs. Nasdaq-100

The above chart already shows how Berkshire's historical alpha has been fading in an era where disruptive innovation drives market leadership. In terms of risk-adjusted returns, Berkshire's more conservative portfolio typically has lower volatility and smaller drawdowns than the Nasdaq-100. However, another tenet in successful growth investing is that those with the stomach to weather volatility reap the greatest harvest at the end. The tech-heavy Nasdaq's tremendous outperformance often more than compensates for its volatility. A chart of rolling 1-year relative returns shows tech beating Berkshire about two-thirds of the time since 2010; Berkshire's wins were largely confined to brief value-stock comebacks or crisis rotations into defensives.

Macro and Strategic Shifts: Old Economy vs. AI Economy

Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio and subsidiaries are heavily weighted toward what could be called the "old economy". This includes insurance (Geico, Gen Re), industrials (Precision Castparts), energy and utilities (Berkshire Hathaway Energy), transportation (BNSF Railroad), and consumer staples (Coca-Cola (KO), See's Candies, etc.). These are generally mature sectors that grow roughly in line with GDP and population over time. The global railroads industry is only expected to grow at around 4-5% annually through 2030. The overall insurance industry is projected to grow only in the low-to-mid single digits (on the order of ~3.5% per year globally) to 2030. In short, core areas where Berkshire excels are steady but growing, and in some cases face secular headwinds. Consider that insurance faces disruptive threats from InsurTech and AI-driven underwriting, railroads could be affected by shifts to renewable energy reducing coal shipments, and consumer brands must adapt to e-commerce and changing consumer preferences.

By contrast, the sectors leading the AI and tech innovation wave (such as semiconductors, cloud computing, artificial intelligence software, and high-tech infrastructure) are expected to grow at multiples of GDP growth. The global semiconductor industry, for instance, is projected to double by 2030 (reaching ~$1 trillion in revenue) at ~6-8% CAGR, more than twice the rate of the general economy. Artificial intelligence as a whole is likely to add about $15.7 trillion in value to global GDP by 2030. AI-related markets are exploding. The AI software market is growing ~30% annually and could reach about $400 billion by 2030. Generative AI in particular is poised to deliver an astronomical 37% CAGR through 2030.

Greg Abel, Buffett's successor as CEO, will be facing this challenge. He will need to navigate a world in which the center of gravity in business is shifting toward technology-driven sectors outside of Berkshire's traditional domain. In light of these dynamics, I believe Buffett calling his good friend Gates and arranging a close mentorship with Abel would be prudent right now.

The Nasdaq-100 giants' model appears superior in an era of rapid change. Berkshire's traditional model produces fantastic results when applied to steady businesses in stable environments, but it is likely to underperform in a period characterized by disruption. This does not inherently make Abel's coming tenure less valuable if he does not take a heavy tech focus, but it does mean that Berkshire should no longer be viewed as a likely alpha engine and instead as a value-preservation conglomerate.

That said, we should give Berkshire credit for its investment in Apple (AAPL), which was 49% of its equity portfolio at the end of 2023. Berkshire also has a small stake in Amazon (AMZN), and a very successful stake in BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY) (OTCPK:BYDDF). However, this still doesn't show great enthusiasm when it comes to picking technology stocks.

10-Year Forward-Looking Outlook

There are three scenarios that I believe are important to consider at this juncture.

In a base case, we will likely see continued tech-driven outperformance. AI and digital innovation will continue at a rapid (even if moderating) pace, and Nasdaq-100 companies will maintain their growth momentum. Under conditions where Abel competently runs the ship with the same established Berkshire philosophy, Berkshire may achieve an annual return of 9-10% over the next decade, whereas it is quite plausible that the Nasdaq-100 will deliver an annualized return of around 15%. This would be broadly consistent with the historical margin by which tech has beaten Berkshire since 2010 (roughly 4-5% per year). As the S&P 500 (SPY) (SPX) is led and dominated by AI stocks, we can also expect Berkshire to underperform in this regard, but perhaps only marginally. Berkshire is definitely supported by a strong cash position for opportunistic value investing, including in private equity markets, which could support ongoing alpha above the S&P 500.

However, we should consider the tech bull-case outcome. The global economy could enter a productivity super-cycle, with tech companies growing rapidly and profit margins expanding. I already price some of this enthusiasm into my ~15% forward Nasdaq-100 10-year CAGR. However, there is some chance that even my growth-acknowledging base case is outperformed by flywheel network effects permeating through society. Under a tech boom, Berkshire's relative alpha would dramatically decline. Its opportunity cost would be very apparent as investors could have done far better in the innovation leaders. We might also see Berkshire's valuation multiple compress relative to tech in such a scenario. Investors could start viewing Berkshire as a staid "utility-like" company (low growth, low excitement) trading at a discount, while tech gets premium multiples.

In the Berkshire bull-case outcome, various factors could cause tech growth to diminish. It's possible that macro catalysts, including geopolitical events and debt dilemmas, cause the market to acknowledge overvaluation in parts of the tech sector. AI benefits may be over-hyped or take longer to materialize, leading to a decade of multiple compression for tech stocks. In a worst-case outcome for tech, the Nasdaq-100 might yield only a mid-to-high single-digit CAGR, perhaps around 7.5%, over the next 10 years. Berkshire could even beat the Nasdaq-100, with its value-oriented and defensive portfolio delivering 8% or more per year. This would represent a major strategic victory for Berkshire's approach, but I can't help but think it is immensely unlikely. Qualitatively, the trend is that tech innovation is capturing a disproportionate share of economic value. Unless Berkshire can find ways to participate, its relative outperformance will be hard to reclaim.

Conclusion: Hold

For investors looking for world-class returns, the takeaway is nuanced. Buffett's value investing tenets are very much timeless; they continue to be the foundation for most capital allocators' downside protection and wisdom for survival. However, the locus of alpha has undeniably shifted toward those who harness innovation and adapt to a digital economy. In this new age, infusing Silicon Valley's dynamism into Berkshire's DNA will make for the best alpha generation. Abel will be facing a dilemma of whether to seek such alpha generation or to preserve Buffett's legacy more modestly. I'm inclined to believe that Abel will choose the latter, and there is nothing wrong with this. However, this opens up a new era for a new type of capitalist conglomerate to emerge, and all eyes will be on the company that ends up filling Berkshire's position in the AI age.

data.title: CommScope: A Value Play Worth Scoping Out
data.description:
CommScope: an ‘A+’ Value Play With Strong Growth Potential

When scoping out Strong Buy stocks with excellent fundamentals, CommScope Holding Company (NASDAQ:COMM) quickly emerges as a top pick. Ranked fourth among all technology stocks and first among communications equipment stocks, the company is leveraging explosive demand for artificial intelligence through its data center infrastructure solutions for hyperscalers among other tech infrastructure services. After taking a hit from volatile markets over the past few months, COMM has engineered a comeback by focusing on high-growth areas like fiber solutions and enterprise Wi-Fi, returning more than 61% over the past 30 days.

As I wrote earlier this week in my piece titled Value Grab: Top 10 Stocks Under $10, tariff turmoil and the recent market downturn are creating buying opportunities for investors who can see past the panic selling and fear to capitalize on long-term growth and value opportunities. Some of the greatest value and growth opportunities can be found in diversifying portfolios with low-priced small-cap stocks, offering bang for your buck.

COMM’s Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.14 significantly outperformed Wall Street’s expectations of $0.07, resulting in a 100% earnings surprise.

EPS estimates for the year ending December 2025 vary between a low of $0.73 and a high of $0.98, suggesting analysts are confident that COMM’s profitability will continue to improve.

Highlights from the group’s excellent Q1 results include a +23% year-over-year increase in net sales to $1.11B and a 159% Y/Y increase in adjusted EBITDA—its fourth consecutive quarter of improvement in the category. As a result, management reiterated 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $1B to $1.05 billion, backed by impressive performance across the business.

Other key highlights include a massive 87% Y/Y increase of its enterprise fiber business to $213M as it capitalized on massive demand for networking infrastructure by hyperscale data centers and the rise of generative AI.

COMM CEO Chuck Treadway was quick to recognize the threat of potential tariffs on the business on its latest earnings call, informing investors that the company forecast a $10-$15 million “growth impact” from retaliatory levies in the second quarter of 2025. Many of the products used by COMM’s Ruckus wireless networking equipment and software business are made in Vietnam and Taiwan, but “a large portion” are currently exempt from tariffs given the Trump administration’s exemptions for certain electrical equipment, he said, adding that around 80% of COMM’s U.S. sales in Q1 were either sourced in the U.S. or are compliant with the current United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement.

“We will mitigate most of the or some of this impact in the second quarter and all of it by the third quarter, and we're going to do that by using our flexible global manufacturing footprint in addition to increasing U.S. capacity that we're bringing on in the second quarter and our supplier base,” said Treadway.

As I noted earlier this week, the U.S. and China’s 90-day truce to reduce tariffs has helped ease investor worries around the subject, and is creating positive tailwinds for AI-leveraging tech companies that could benefit COMM in the long run.

COMM Leads the Pack by Performance and Quant Grades

While tariff fears may have become apparent during the tech slump earlier this year, they no longer appear to be priced into COMM’s performance. The tech infrastructure provider was upgraded from a Hold to a Strong Buy by Seeking Alpha’s Quant System on April 7, powered by an improved valuation and higher growth prospects. It currently outperforms fellow mid-cap players and tech giants alike as its connectivity solutions become an increasingly integral part of the AI supply chain.

COMM Quant Rating History (as of 5/15/25)

COMM’s biggest publicly traded rivals in the networking infrastructure and solutions space include Cisco (CSCO), Ubiquiti (UI), Juniper Networks (JNPR), Extreme Networks (EXTR). Taiwanese networking equipment manufacturer D-Link (2332.TW) and China’s Huawei are among its largest foreign competitors.

COMM & Networking Infrastructure Peers 1Y Performance vs. S&P 500 (as of 5/15/25)

In addition to providing infrastructure solutions such as cabling and fiber optics that help satisfy the intense workloads of AI data centers, COMM also offers communication application programming interfaces [APIs] and services via its Azure API Management Portal. These services play a different but just as critical role in data center management and infrastructure by enabling automation and data integration. COMM’s top competitors in the API space include Twilio (TWLO), Bandwidth (BAND), Microsoft’s Azure Communication Services platform (MSFT), and Amazon’s Amazon Web Services Communication Developer Services (AMZN).

COMM & API Peers 1Y Performance vs. S&P 500 (as of 5/15/25)

From a Quant perspective, COMM outperforms all of its key rivals in terms of valuation, growth, and overall quant rating. It is a top performer in terms of EPS revisions and momentum, and boasts the highest performance on a 1M, YTD, and 1Y basis. It also has the lowest forward price-to-earnings growth ratio among the stocks with available Quant data on that particular metric.

COMM Beats Top Networking Infrastructure & API Peers

Let’s dive deeper into COMM’s Quant Factor Ratings.

CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (NASDAQ: COMM)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.25B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/15/25): 4 out of 537

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/15/25): 1 out of 41

Despite returning a whopping 406% over the past year, COMM still touts ‘A+’ valuation grade with a forward GAAP P/E of 4.75, an 83% discount from the sector median. Its forward PEG of 0.16 is an impressive 90% discount from the sector median.

It also demonstrates excellent growth with Y/Y EBIT growth of 2,003%, a sector-crushing 17,942.7% difference from the median. Y/Y EBITDA growth of 61.5% tops the sector’s 8.1% median by a whopping 658.4%, while EPS long-term growth comes in at a 215% difference to the sector.

Profitability is solid, with return on total assets [TTM] OF 10.6%, a 401% difference to the 2.13% sector median and $263M in cash from operations. COMM’s CAPEX/Sales ratio, however, could use some improvement at a -63% difference from the sector.

Momentum has remained fantastic even in the grips of a wider tech downturn, with three-month price performance of nearly 14%, significantly above the sector’s -8.5%.

And with five upward EPS revisions and 5 upward revenue revisions over the past 90 days, analysts appear in line with SA Quant’s Strong Buy assessment.

Risks to Consider

Despite COMM’s turbocharged growth, reported free cash flow in Q1 of -$202.4M, a number it attributed to the rapid expansion of its Ruckus unit, among other projects. “As we look at cash flow guidance for 2025, we still expect breakeven cash flow,” Treadway told investors during its Q1 earnings call. I believe this debt is manageable in the long term, given the unstoppable demand for hyperscale data centers and the connectivity solutions required to power those data centers, but its current debt pile is worth highlighting.

Conclusion: Plug Into a Long-Term Value Strategy With COMM

COMM’s incredible performance over the past year, incredible momentum, and powerful long-term growth outlook make it a compelling choice given its ultra-low share price. However, its large debt pile means that potential investors should only consider COMM if they’re in it for the long haul and are willing to wait for the company to improve its profitability over time. The rise of generative AI and other technologies require a vast network of underlying connectivity infrastructure, and COMM has demonstrated that it is a top player in this fast-growing subsector.

Alternatively, if you're seeking a limited number of monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks, consider exploring Alpha Picks. Alpha Picks employs a transparent and systematic strategy that combines the expertise of Seeking Alpha’s Quantitative Team, our unique data, and the computational power of the systems we have built. Let SA’s Quant Team do the research for you in building a market-beating, data-driven portfolio for long-term growth.

data.title: Alibaba's Post-Earnings Selloff Is An Overreaction
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) just released its March quarter earnings. The release missed on both revenue and EPS, triggering a major selloff in BABA stock. At the time of this writing, BABA was down about 8% for the day.

Alibaba’s miss was partially the product of high expectations, and partially the result of a poor performance in the company's Cainiao Smart Logistics subsidiary, which shrank 12%.

Going into the release, analysts were expecting big things from Alibaba. The consensus revenue estimate was $33.32 billion while the adjusted earnings (EPS) estimate was $1.78. Both of these estimates implied high year-over-year growth. In the end, Alibaba missed on both. However, it’s important to know that the expectations were high and trending higher in the days and weeks leading up to the release. In the 90 days leading up to its release, Alibaba saw 24 up revisions and only three down revisions. This implies that analysts were growing increasingly optimistic in the weeks leading up to the release, and possibly getting a bit ahead of themselves.

I too was optimistic heading into Alibaba’s earnings release. A few days before Alibaba reported, its competitor JD.com (JD) reported and widely beat estimates. The same factors that drove JD’s beat (i.e., China’s consumption stimulus) applied to Alibaba as well. Ultimately, I was happy with the results that Alibaba put out, although they failed to match the numbers that I had estimated in my recent earnings preview article.

Among the most impressive things in Alibaba’s earnings release was the cloud segment’s performance. This segment had long been underperforming, with low single digit growth in past quarters. This time around, it was a completely different story, with high and accelerating top line growth and a decent EBIT (or rather EBITA) margin.

Alibaba’s cloud segment has been the focus of much attention lately–in the U.S. as well as in China. It is the segment that is primarily responsible for developing Alibaba’s generative AI models, such as the Qwen language model and the Wan video creation/editing app. Qwen currently ranks sixth in Huggingface’s open LLM leaderboard; it ranks higher in math and coding. Like many AI stocks, Alibaba has been getting bid up in the markets lately. The cloud segment’s March quarter showing proved that the hype has been well deserved.

When I last covered Alibaba, I said that the stock was a Strong Buy and that I was doubling down on it ahead of earnings. Since then, the stock has put out a solid earnings release and released several new AI models. The new developments merit further coverage. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will explain why I consider Alibaba stock a buy after earnings.

Earnings Recap

Alibaba’s recent earnings release showed considerable growth in revenue and earnings, although less than what markets had expected. Some highlight metrics included:

  • $32.58 billion in revenue, up 7% (miss by $580 million).

  • $3.9 billion in operating income, up 93%.

  • $1.7 billion in reported net income, up 1,203%.

  • $4.1 billion in adjusted net income, up 22%.

  • $0.71 in reported EPS, up 294% (miss by $0.42).

  • $1.73 in adjusted EPS, up 23% (miss by $0.04).

  • $516 million in free cash flow (FCF), down 76%.

A quick note on the differences in growth rates between net income and EPS (specifically the former growing much faster than the latter). This was not due to an increase in share count–Alibaba’s share count actually declined 5.1% over the last 12 months. It was, rather, the product of a large difference in “net income” and “net income attributable to ordinary shareholders.” “Net income to ordinary shareholders” was much higher than net income in the year ago quarter, and was the metric used to calculate diluted EPS. It’s for this reason that whole company net income grew faster than diluted EPS in the quarter just reported.

Overall, the results Alibaba put out were below both my expectations and Wall Street’s expectations. However, when we look into Alibaba’s segment results, we can see clearly the reason for the miss–and it has nothing to do with Alibaba’s most lucrative, money making segments.

Below are the top and bottom line results from Alibaba’s various segments (not including very small ones like digital media and local services).

  • China commerce: $13.97 billion in revenue (up 9%); $5.7 billion in EBIT (up 8%).

  • International commerce: $4.62 billion in revenue (up 22%); $-492 million in EBIT (up 13%).

  • The cloud: $4.15 billion in revenue (up 18%); $333 million in EBITA (up 69%).

  • Cainiao Smart logistics: $2.9 billion in revenue (down 12%); $-53 million in EBITA (up 55%).

As you can see, most of Alibaba’s segments actually grew their revenue much faster than the 7% whole company growth rate. It was largely the 12% decline in Cainiao smart logistics that drove the top line miss. Likewise, the losses at Cainiao and international commerce contributed to the bottom line miss. If earnings were calculated using only the China commerce and cloud segments, then BABA would have beaten on the bottom line in Q1.

The earnings miss was, therefore, partially a product of Alibaba investing heavily in growth opportunities (international commerce is by far the company’s biggest grower). The top line miss was attributable to Alibaba’s e-commerce businesses taking over logistics functions from Cainiao. I’d call that a technicality and not something that investors should be seriously worried about.

How the Recent Earnings Release Impacts Alibaba’s Valuation

In my recent earnings preview article, I called Alibaba stock cheap, particularly in comparison to the global IT sector (i.e., publicly traded tech companies in China and abroad). The company’s recent earnings release impacted the valuation in two ways:

  1. Revenue and earnings increased.

  2. The stock price went down.

As a result, Alibaba stock is now cheaper than it was when I last covered it. Here are the key TTM figures:

  • Revenue: $137.3 billion.

  • Common shareholder’s equity: $139.16 billion.

  • Reported EPS: $7.38.

  • Adjusted EPS: $9.01.

  • 2.39 billion shares outstanding.

  • $57.44 in revenue per share.

  • $58.22 in book value per share.

From these figures, we get the following valuation multiples using $123.61 (the price at the time of this writing) as the stock price:

  • Price/sales: 2.15.

  • Price/book: 2.12.

  • GAAP P/E: 16.74.

  • Adjusted P/E: 13.72.

As you can see, these multiples are a little lower than the ones Seeking Alpha Quant currently has on file. This demonstrates that Alibaba’s earnings growth and post-earnings selloff made its stock cheaper. Therefore, Alibaba remains cheaper than its comps in the global IT sector, with an even bigger discount than it had when I last covered it. This makes it a buy in my books.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Alibaba’s March Quarter earnings release is that it was good, but not as good as investors had hoped for. Revenue and earnings were a little lower than expected, but there was considerable strength in several subsidiaries--especially the cloud. On the whole, I think this post-earnings selloff is a buying opportunity. However, I am downgrading my rating to Buy from Strong Buy, as the reported numbers were less than what I was hoping for.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
